welcome_message: Welcome to BoxLings! We'll teach you BoxLang AND test-driven development. For exercises with tests, read the test file first to understand what your code should do!
final_message: Congratulations! You've mastered BoxLang fundamentals. Keep practicing and building amazing things with BoxLang!

exercises.name: intro1
exercises.hint: This exercise is just to verify your setup. Enter 'n' to move on to the next exercise.

exercises.name: intro2
exercises.hint: The compiler is telling you that the function name is wrong. Read the error message carefully - or use the IDE to give you suggestions!

exercises.name: variables1
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, you need the 'var' keyword to declare variables inside functions.

exercises.name: variables2
exercises.hint: BoxLang uses type inference - it can figure out the type from the value you assign. Try giving 'x' a value!

exercises.name: variables3
exercises.hint: Read the test file first! It tells you exactly what values to use. The test expects x=10, y=20, and sum=30.

exercises.name: variables4
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, variables are dynamically typed - you can reassign them to different types!

exercises.name: variables5
exercises.hint: Shadowing allows you to reuse variable names. Check the test to see what the final value should be.

exercises.name: variables6
exercises.hint: Constants in BoxLang... well, BoxLang doesn't have true constants like other languages. Variables can always be reassigned!

exercises.name: functions1
exercises.hint: This main function is calling a function that doesn't exist yet. You need to create a function called call_me() that prints a message. Functions in BoxLang are declared using the 'function' keyword.

exercises.name: functions2
exercises.hint: The greet() function is trying to use a variable called 'name', but it doesn't have access to it. You need to add 'name' as a parameter to the function signature.

exercises.name: functions3
exercises.hint: Your function calculates the sum but doesn't return it! Functions need the 'return' keyword to send a value back to the caller.

exercises.name: functions4
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, you can provide default values for parameters using the = syntax in the function signature. This way, if the argument isn't provided, the default is used.

exercises.name: functions5
exercises.hint: BoxLang has two types of function expressions: Closures (=>) and Lambdas (->). Closures use the FAT ARROW (=>) and can access variables from their surrounding scope. Lambdas use the skinny arrow (->) and are pure functions.

exercises.name: functions6
exercises.hint: When using array methods like map(), filter(), reduce(), you typically use LAMBDAS (->), not closures (=>). Lambdas are pure functions that just transform data without side effects.

exercises.name: conditionals1
exercises.hint: Your function only returns a value when a > b. What happens when b is bigger or they're equal? You need an else clause!

exercises.name: conditionals2
exercises.hint: The ternary operator is BoxLang's shorthand for if/else. Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

exercises.name: conditionals3
exercises.hint: Your switch handles weekdays but not weekends! Add cases for Saturday and Sunday.

exercises.name: conditionals4
exercises.hint: Think carefully about AND vs OR. For voting: BOTH conditions must be true. For teenagers: age must be BETWEEN 13 AND 19.

exercises.name: datatypes1
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, the + operator is for numbers. Use & (ampersand) to concatenate strings!

exercises.name: datatypes2
exercises.hint: Use int() to convert a decimal to an integer, or use the backslash operator for integer division.

exercises.name: datatypes3
exercises.hint: The string 'true' is not the same as the boolean true. Remove the quotes!

exercises.name: datatypes4
exercises.hint: Use dateAdd() to add time to a date. Format: dateAdd('d', amount, date)

exercises.name: datatypes5
exercises.hint: Arrays use square brackets [ ], not curly braces { }!

exercises.name: datatypes6
exercises.hint: Structs use curly braces { }, not square brackets [ ]! And use : not = for key-value pairs.

exercises.name: datatypes7
exercises.hint: Ordered structs use square brackets [ ] with key:value syntax to preserve order!

exercises.name: datatypes8
exercises.hint: Use type-checking functions: isArray(), isStruct(), isNumeric(), isBoolean(), etc.

exercises.name: arrays1
exercises.hint: Arrays in BoxLang start at index 1, not 0! This is different from most languages.

exercises.name: arrays2
exercises.hint: Use the append() member function to add items to an array.

exercises.name: arrays3
exercises.hint: When iterating with each(), use a closure (=>) not a lambda (->).

exercises.name: arrays4
exercises.hint: Remember: BoxLang arrays are 1-based! First element is [1], last is [length].

exercises.name: scopes1
exercises.hint: Unscoped variables in scripts go to the 'variables' scope. Access them with variables.x

exercises.name: scopes2
exercises.hint: Variables inside functions need the 'var' keyword to be in local scope.

exercises.name: scopes3
exercises.hint: Function parameters are available in the 'arguments' scope. Access them with arguments.name

exercises.name: scopes4
exercises.hint: In classes: 'variables' is private, 'this' is public. Properties go to variables scope.

exercises.name: scopes5
exercises.hint: The server scope persists for the entire BoxLang runtime. Run the script multiple times to see!

exercises.name: structs1
exercises.hint: Check the struct definition - what keys does it actually have? Use the correct property name.

exercises.name: structs2
exercises.hint: BoxLang struct keys are case-insensitive! NAME, name, NaMe all refer to the same key.

exercises.name: structs3
exercises.hint: Use count() to get the number of keys, and keyExists() to check if a key exists.

exercises.name: structs4
exercises.hint: Nested structs are accessed with dot notation: outer.inner.property

exercises.name: structs5
exercises.hint: Ordered structs use square brackets [ ] with key:value syntax, not curly braces { }.

exercises.name: strings1
exercises.hint: BoxLang uses ## for expression interpolation, not $ like some other languages.

exercises.name: strings2
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, just continue your string on the next line - no special syntax needed!

exercises.name: strings3
exercises.hint: Use ucase() for uppercase and trim() to remove whitespace.

exercises.name: strings4
exercises.hint: The & operator concatenates strings. The + operator is for numbers!

exercises.name: strings5
exercises.hint: Use listToArray( string, delimiter ) to convert a delimited string to an array.

exercises.name: strings6
exercises.hint: Escape quotes by doubling them: "" or use single quotes on the outside.

exercises.name: imports1
exercises.hint: Use the import statement at the top of your class: import ClassName

exercises.name: imports2
exercises.hint: Java classes need the java: prefix. Example: import java:java.util.ArrayList

exercises.name: imports3
exercises.hint: When using an alias, create instances with the alias name: new AliasName()

exercises.name: imports4
exercises.hint: After importing, create an instance with: new HashMap()
